background.domain_detail: Plant operations manager at a mid-size stamping and fabrication plant — 200 people, 3 shifts. Responsible for uptime, throughput, quality escapes, and labor efficiency. Spent 20 years on the floor before moving into management.

build.name: Manufacturing AI Operations
build.description: Built and deployed two AI systems for his own plant. First: a downtime tracking and predictive alert system — tracks machine states, logs unplanned stops, sends Telegram alerts to shift supervisors before failures cascade. Reduced unplanned downtime 23% over 6 months. Second: a quality escape classifier — photographs defects on the line, uses vision AI to classify them by type and root cause, replaces handwritten shift logs. Both are live. Now wants to replicate these for other manufacturers and charge for it as an AI operations consultant.
shipped: Downtime tracking + predictive alert system — 200-person stamping plant, live 6 months, 23% reduction in unplanned downtime
Quality escape classifier using vision AI — live 3 months, replacing manual defect logging on 2 production lines
build.target_user: Operations director at a mid-size manufacturer losing $50k+ per month on unplanned downtime with no systematic visibility into why

goals.title: First real external conversation
goals.description: Have one real conversation with an operations director at a company I don't currently work for — no pitch, just ask about their downtime problem and listen.

action_steps.text: Write three sentences describing what you do, as if talking to a stranger who has never heard of your previous career. Use present tense. Don't qualify. Don't mention plant manager.
action_steps.coach_reason: identity_anchor pattern active — first exercise establishes present-tense identity without the old title as a crutch
action_steps.completion_note: Took me 20 minutes. First 5 attempts all started with 'I used to be...' or 'I come from...' Finally got to: 'I build AI systems that predict and prevent unplanned downtime in manufacturing plants. I've deployed two systems that are running live right now. I'm doing this for other companies.' Felt uncomfortable to write. Also felt true.

action_steps.text: Write one paragraph about your current build using only your domain expertise — no technical language, no mention of AI or code. What does your 22 years on the floor tell you about the problem you're solving that most people selling to manufacturing would miss?
action_steps.coach_reason: identity_anchor level 2 — shift from 'I'm not technical enough' to 'my floor knowledge is the product'
action_steps.completion_note: Easier than expected once I stopped trying to sound like a developer. Wrote about how shift supervisors don't trust dashboard data they didn't generate themselves, and why every AI tool for manufacturing fails if it doesn't account for the 2am culture on a plant floor. That's not something you learn from a laptop.

action_steps.text: Write a one-paragraph bio that leads with what you're building and what you know — not where you came from. Your 22 years appears only if it directly explains why you're the right person to build this. Then post it in the Unlabeled community.
action_steps.coach_reason: identity_anchor level 3 — real publication in a safe space, bio leads with the build not the title
action_steps.completion_note: Posted it. Someone in the community replied asking if I'd talked to companies in automotive tier 1. First time anyone external has shown interest. The bio that led with 'I build AI systems for manufacturing operations' got a response. The seven bios I wrote that started with 'Former plant manager' got nothing because I never sent them.

action_steps.text: Use the bio in a real introduction. Find one operations director or plant manager on LinkedIn you don't know personally. Send them the bio — not as a pitch, as an introduction. 'I build X for Y. Thought it might be relevant to what you're working on.' That's it. No ask.
action_steps.coach_reason: identity_anchor level 4 — first use of the new identity in a direct professional interaction outside a safe space

active_commitment.text: Post a 3-sentence description of the downtime tracking system on LinkedIn — what it does, the result (23% reduction in unplanned downtime), and that I am now available to build this for other manufacturers. By Thursday May 28.
active_commitment.share_post: Built an AI alert system for our plant floor 6 months ago. Machine states tracked in real time, shift supervisors get a Telegram message before a failure cascades. Result: 23% reduction in unplanned downtime. I'm now available to build this for other manufacturers. If you're losing shifts to equipment failures you didn't see coming, let's talk.
active_commitment.print_card: Post the downtime system on LinkedIn by Thursday May 28. — May 22, 2026

daily_reminders.day_1: You have the 23% number. You have the live system. What's the first sentence?
daily_reminders.day_2: Have you drafted the post yet? The shift supervisor story is in there — use it.
daily_reminders.day_3: What's stopping you from posting the draft you already have?
daily_reminders.day_4: Someone on a plant floor right now is dealing with the exact problem your system solves. They can't find you if this isn't posted.
daily_reminders.day_5: Thursday is tomorrow. What version could you post in the next hour?
daily_reminders.day_6: Today. What time are you posting?
daily_reminders.day_7: Did you post it? What happened in the first 24 hours?

commitment_history.text: Post in the Unlabeled community — describe the quality escape classifier: what it does, how it works, what changed on the line. Three sentences. By May 20.
commitment_history.outcome_notes: Did it. Posted Tuesday night. Three people responded within 24 hours. One asked if I'd worked with food manufacturing. I realized I'd been sitting on this for months for no reason. The fear was bigger than the actual risk.

commitment_history.text: Send my new bio to one person from my professional network — not a friend, someone I respect but don't know well. Just the bio, no ask. By May 18.
commitment_history.outcome_notes: Didn't do it. Kept rewriting the bio. Got to version 7. None of them felt right. Classic — the identity anchor pattern doing exactly what the coach said it would. The bio wasn't the problem.

goals.title: First paid engagement
goals.description: Close one paid engagement outside my current employer — an AI operations audit for a manufacturer, $5,000 to $15,000. Doesn't have to be perfect. Has to be real.

goals.title: Full-time consultant
goals.description: I have left the plant manager role. I am billing $20,000+ per month as an AI operations consultant for manufacturers. When someone asks what I do, I say consultant — not former plant manager.

avoidance_language: I need to frame it right first
I don't want to look like I'm pretending to be something I'm not
engagement_triggers: describing the 23% downtime reduction number — he speaks faster
explaining why floor experience makes him different from every other AI consultant selling to manufacturing
talking about the shift supervisor who said the alerts changed how he runs nights

publishing_log.description: First public post about the quality escape classifier — what it does, how defect photos get classified automatically, what changed on the line. Three sentences as committed.
Posted the new bio in the Unlabeled community — leads with 'I build AI systems for manufacturing operations,' no mention of plant manager title. Got a reply asking about automotive tier 1.

coach_notes: [2026-05-14]: Phase 0 complete. 22 years manufacturing operations, plant manager level. Parallel builder — still employed. Shipped two live AI systems: downtime tracker (23% improvement, 6 months live) and quality escape classifier (3 months live). These are not prototypes — they are running on a real floor with real results. Identity pattern is clear and strong: he introduces himself as 'former plant manager' even when describing tools that are actively deployed and producing measurable results. Resistance pattern: identity_anchor. Dominant lens: business. [2026-05-17]: Reflection phase done on 30d goal. Two contradictions surfaced: (1) More deployed AI work than 90% calling themselves AI consultants — but introduces as plant manager. (2) 30-day goal requires external conversation, but has published nothing. Both accepted without defensiveness. [2026-05-19]: Clarity reached. One-sentence: 'I build AI systems that prevent unplanned downtime and quality escapes in manufacturing plants.' Named what makes him different: 22 years on the floor at 2am. That is embedded in the product. [2026-05-20]: Resistance phase done. Named identity_anchor explicitly. R9 card deployed. Action step 3 (bio in community) completed — got a reply. Pattern cracking. [2026-05-22]: 30d goal at commitment phase. LinkedIn post declared — specific, 23% number, by May 28. Rung 5. Previous missed commitment (bio to network contact) debriefed: seven rewrites, never sent.
